topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a poorly aimed stone. A single, sterile sentence: "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise celebration for Alex has been cancelled." My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles ache. I stared at the screen, the cheerful decorations I’d mentally conjured—the balloons, the embarrassing slideshow—suddenly feeling like cruel mockery. I immediately opened my phone, the screen illuminating a cold, hard knot in my stomach. I didn't call anyone. Instead, I started pacing the small apartment, the rhythm of my footsteps sharp and agitated against the hardwood floor. Every surface seemed to reflect the disappointment, the sheer waste of effort. I picked up the remnants of the party planning notes, crumpling them slowly in my fist until the paper was a tight, angry ball.

[story 2]

"Cancelled." The word echoed in the silence of the room, sharp and brittle. I felt a slow burn starting low in my chest, a heat that had nothing to do with anger and everything to do with profound irritation. I leaned back in my chair, letting out a breath that was more of a scoff than a sigh. I looked around the living room, cataloging the remnants of the effort: the half-made cake box, the string of fairy lights still coiled neatly. It was a deliberate snub. I stood up, my movements stiff, and walked over to the window, staring out at the indifferent streetlights. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat. I didn't bother looking for a phone or an outlet; I just wanted to feel the injustice physically. I slammed my palm against the windowpane, the glass vibrating slightly under the force of my frustration.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I didn't even need to read the details fully. The implication hung in the air, thick and suffocating. A tight, unpleasant clenching began in my gut, spreading outwards like a cold tide. I walked into the kitchen, my gaze fixed on the pristine countertop. Everything looked too neat, too organized, a perfect facade masking this sudden deflation. I picked up a glass of water, turning it over and over in my hands, the ice clinking with an irritating sharpness. I took a long, deliberate sip, letting the coolness barely mask the simmering resentment. I set the glass down with unnecessary force, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet space. I made a point of straightening every misplaced item, an aggressive attempt to impose order on the chaos the cancellation had wrought.
